title: Documentation for ARC
text:
Welcome
This web book is a collection of general documentation, demostrations, and other supporting materials for John Curtin’s Addiction Research Center.

title: 1  CHTC
text:
1.1 Common UNIX Commands

condor_submit sub.sub
condor_q for quick review of queue for submitted jobs
condor_q -hold for quick review of held jobs
condor_q -af HoldReason for more detailed review of held jobs
condor_qedit [cluster id number] RequestMemory [memory] to increase memory. Example: condor_qedit 16892087 RequestMemory 20000. Can specific specific job with .# added to the ID. Memory is specified in MB in our training control files.
condor_release $USER to release all held jobs for user (e.g., after increasing memory)
condor_rm [jobid] or condor_rm $USER to remove a job or all jobs
condor_history [NetID] -limit 10 -af requestmemory memoryusage is an example of reviewing recent job history (past 10 in this example) for a subset of the history parameters. Can list all the parameters (long format) for a single job using condor_history 16892169.1 -l where the jobid is listed explicitly.
ls -lSr to list files sorted by size (with larger sizes later). Useful to find the non-zero error files
nano job_nums.txt to edit job_nums.txt for jobs to re-run due to errors
cat results_* > results_all.csv to combine all results into one file before transferring back to our server
chmod +x get_missing_jobs.sh to change permissions on get_missing_jobs.sh to run it. ./get_missing_jobs.sh to run it.

title: 1  CHTC
text:
1.1 CHTC Office Hours & Live Help
See the Get Help Page. Rachel and Christine are our two main support people at CHTC. You can email them at chtc@cs.wisc.edu
The zoom link for CHTC office hours is go.wisc.edu/chtc-officehours. Office hours are current scheduled at * Tuesday morning: 10:30am - 12:00pm * Thursday afternoon: 3:00 - 4:30 pm.

title: 1  CHTC
text:
1.2 Logging In

Open PuTTY. Enter Hostname: submit-1.chtc.wisc.edu, Port: 22, connection type: ssh
Log in with your netid. You will have to use DUO for MFA.
Open WinSCP. Use the same credentials as PuTTY. You can drag files between your computer and CHTC. You can also edit documents with this program.

title: 1  CHTC
text:
1.3 Hello World and General Help
For substantial help, you can start with the CHTC home page and their list of how to docs for HTC.
CHTC provides a Hello World overview of how to use CHTC. It is worth a review for new users.

title: 1  CHTC
text:
1.4 Software
On windows, use PuTTY for secure shell connection (SSH) to CHTC submit server and WinSCP for FTP.

title: 1  CHTC
text:
1.7 Making package TARS
CHTC offers a detailed step-by-step walkthrough for making package tars.
In our CHTC workflow we have two tars located at P:/chtc/tars

features.tar
This tar contains all the packages needed for making features on CHTC.

Current packages in this tar include:
10/4/22 - recreated TAR to work with updated CHTC Linux OS (v 8) and R version 4.1.3 (updated from 4.0.2)

As of 6/1/23 Current packages include:
lubridate - 1.8.0,
vroom - 1.6.0, 
dplyr - 1.0.10,
stringr - 1.4.1,
foreach -1.5.2,
tis - 1.39

train.tar
This tar contains all the packages needed for fitting model configurations on CHTC.

Current packages in this tar include:
10/4/22 - recreated TAR to work with updated CHTC Linux OS (v 8) and R version 4.1.3 (updated from 4.0.2)    
Note: with update no longer need to use compiler fix-around for installing glmnet and xgboost (see demo_changing_package_compiler in demos_docs/chtc for how we got around this error)

As of 6/1/23 Current packages include:
readr - 2.1.3, 
dplyr - 1.0.10, 
tidyr - 1.2.1, 
recipes - 1.0.1, 
parsnip - 1.0.2, 
themis - 1.0.0, 
tune - 1.0.0, 
yardstick - 1.1.0, 
rsample - 1.1.0, 
ranger - 0.14.1, 
psych - 2.2.9, 
purrr - 1.0.1, 
kknn - 1.3.1, 
vip - 0.3.2, 
glmnet - 4.1.4,
stringr - 1.4.1, 
vroom - 1.6.0,
foreach - 1.5.2, 
lubridate - 1.8.0,
xgboost - 1.6.0.1
iml 0.11.1

title: 1  CHTC
text:
1.8 Open Science Pool
JJC plans to get an account directly for the OS Pool. We can access this pools through flock/glide BUT with an account, we can run twice as many jobs (using each account separately). May still just be easier to run using multiple lab accounts on CHTC but worth considering.

title: 1  CHTC
text:
1.9 Common CONDOR commands

condor_submit sub.sub
condor_q for quick review of queue for submitted jobs
condor_q -hold for quick review of held jobs
condor_q -af HoldReason for more detailed review of held jobs
condor_qedit [cluster id number] RequestMemory [memory] to increase memory. Example: condor_qedit 16892087 RequestMemory 20000. Can specific specific job with .# added to the ID. Memory is specified in MB in our training control files.
condor_release $USER to release all held jobs for user (e.g., after increasing memory)
condor_rm [jobid] or condor_rm $USER to remove a job or all jobs
condor_history [NetID] -limit 10 -af requestmemory memoryusage is an example of reviewing recent job history (past 10 in this example) for a subset of the history parameters. Can list all the parameters (long format) for a single job using condor_history 16892169.1 -l where the jobid is listed explicitly.

condor_hold $USER to hold all jobs running (e.g., to increase memory).

title: 1  CHTC
text:
1.10 Common UNIX generic commands

ls -lSr to list files sorted by size (with larger sizes later). Useful to find the non-zero error files. Can have issues when applied to large numbers of files. See use of find and xargs below if needed.
rm to remove file(s). rm -r to remove non-empty directory
nano is used to edit text files. For example nano job_nums.txt to edit job_nums.txt for jobs to re-run due to errors
cat results_* > results_all.csv to combine all results into one file before transferring back to our server
chmod +x get_missing_jobs.sh to change permissions on get_missing_jobs.sh to run it. ./get_missing_jobs.sh to run it.
find -maxdepth 1 -name "results_*.csv" | wc -l will find results_*.csv in local folder (maxdepth 1) and pipe them into a word count that counts lines (-l)
cat job_nums.txt | wc -l reads contents of job_nums.text and pipes to a word count that counts lines (-l)
find -maxdepth 1 -name "results_*.csv" | ls -lSr gets around the too many arguments issue.

head -n +1 results_1.csv > all_results.csv; find -maxdepth 1 -name "results_*.csv" | xargs awk 'FNR>1' >> all_results.csv to concatenate results files (including only one header row). See tutorial on awk to understand its use for simple programming. See a tutorial on the use of xargs.

title: 1  CHTC
text:
1.5 General CHTC Workflow

1.5.1 Making Batch of Jobs


1.5.2 Testing Jobs


1.5.3 Running jobs

Copy files that are created by mak_jobs() from the server to CHTC submit server.
Confirm that the EOL character for execute.sh have been changed to Unix. The EOL can be changed using Notepad++. From the menu, select Edit:EOL conversion and choose Unix (LF)
Confirm that you have an error directory. If not, create one (mkdir error)
Type condor_submit sub.sub
Type condor_q to confirm that the number of jobs that were submitted matches your expectations



1.5.4 Monitoring Running Jobs

run condor_q to monitor progress on your jobs.
run condor_q -hold to explore the reason for held jobs. If necessary, you may need to change the requested memory or disk space to get those jobs to complete. See below for more detail. condor_q -af HoldReason will provide more detail on why a job is held if needed.
condor_release $USER will release all held jobs for the user. You can substitute batch or job number to release a subset of held jobs if needed
condor_rm $USER will remove/cancel all jobs for the user.
You can review the error files associated with the batch of jobs to detect and understand errors as well. Change to the error directory (cd error). You can sort the files so that the files with errors (i.e., file switch file size > 0) display last by typing ls -lSr If there are MANY (> 60K) files, you may need to pipe the file names into ls by typing find -maxdepth 1 -name "err_*.err" | ls -lSr. You can view the contents of a non-zero error file using nano. e.g., nano err_1111.err



1.5.5 Editing Running Jobs
At times some (or all) jobs may be held if they require more memory or disk space than was requested in sub.sub. You can update these parameters and then release the held jobs.

To update memory, type condor_qedit [batch_name or job_id] RequestMemory [memory]. You can see the batch_name and job_ids by typing condor_q -hold Memory is quantified in MB.

To update disk space, type condor_qedit [batch_name or job_id] RequestDisk [space]. You can see the batch_name and job_ids by typing condor_q -hold Space is quantified in MB.

Type condor_release $USER to release all held jobs from the user.
If you expect that you will need the specified higher memory or disk space for the currently running jobs (not yet held), you can hold them by typing condor_hold $USER and then release them with condor_release $USER such that they will now find machine with the higher levels for these parameters.



1.5.6 Transferring Jobs back to Our Server
head -n +1 results_1.csv > all_results.csv; find -maxdepth 1 -name "results_*.csv" | xargs awk 'FNR>1' >> all_results.csv
